topics.title: NP-completeness and reductions
topics.rationale: The clearest lens for seeing when many different-looking problems share one computational obstruction. This is the backbone for writing notes that move between intuition, formal reductions, and proof obligations.

sources.label: Stephen Cook, The Complexity of Theorem-Proving Procedures
Richard Karp, Reducibility Among Combinatorial Problems

topics.title: Shortest paths and graph structure
topics.rationale: Shortest path algorithms expose the connection between invariants, data structures, and graph modeling. They also make implementation tradeoffs concrete without losing the mathematical thread.

sources.label: Edsger W. Dijkstra, A note on two problems in connexion with graphs
Robert Tarjan, Data Structures and Network Algorithms

topics.title: Depth-first search and graph decompositions
topics.rationale: DFS is small enough to teach carefully and powerful enough to generate bridges, components, topological order, and linear-time graph structure. It rewards exact invariants.

sources.label: Robert Tarjan, Depth-first search and linear graph algorithms

topics.title: Network flow and cuts
topics.rationale: Flow turns algorithm design into a conversation between primal constructions and dual certificates. It is one of the best places to show why correctness proofs can feel inevitable.

sources.label: L. R. Ford and D. R. Fulkerson, Maximal Flow Through a Network

topics.title: Dynamic programming and optimal substructure
topics.rationale: Dynamic programming is where informal pattern recognition needs discipline: state definitions, recurrence validity, boundary cases, and reconstruction all have to line up.

topics.title: Space-bounded computation
topics.rationale: Space complexity changes the shape of what a computation is allowed to remember. It is a useful counterweight to time-only algorithm discussions.

sources.label: Walter Savitch, Relationships between nondeterministic and deterministic tape complexities

topics.title: Primality and randomized thinking
topics.rationale: Primality testing is a strong route into randomized algorithms, derandomization questions, and the surprise value of a deterministic polynomial-time proof.

sources.label: Manindra Agrawal, Neeraj Kayal, and Nitin Saxena, PRIMES is in P

topics.title: Matrix methods and fast algebra
topics.rationale: Fast algebra is a reminder that basic operations hide deep algorithmic structure. It creates a bridge from algorithms into complexity and numerical computation.

sources.label: Volker Strassen, Gaussian elimination is not optimal

topics.title: Amortized data structures
topics.rationale: Amortized analysis teaches readers to reason about sequences rather than isolated operations. It is one of the best techniques for turning messy behavior into a crisp bound.

sources.label: Daniel Sleator and Robert Tarjan, Self-Adjusting Binary Search Trees

topics.title: Algorithms as a discipline
topics.rationale: The site needs a stable center of gravity: precise algorithm statements, correctness arguments, complexity analysis, and implementation realism in the same frame.

sources.label: Thomas H. Cormen, Charles E. Leiserson, Ronald L. Rivest, and Clifford Stein, Introduction to Algorithms

topics.title: Randomized algorithms
topics.rationale: Randomness makes probability a design tool rather than a post-hoc analysis layer. It is useful for explaining expected bounds, concentration, and failure probability.

sources.label: Rajeev Motwani and Prabhakar Raghavan, Randomized Algorithms

topics.title: Computational complexity as a map
topics.rationale: Complexity theory gives the notes a shared coordinate system for reductions, complete problems, randomized classes, space bounds, and what counts as evidence of hardness.

sources.label: Sanjeev Arora and Boaz Barak, Computational Complexity: A Modern Approach
